text: The video shows a laparoscopic cholecystectomy being performed using the standard four-port technique, with trocars placed for optimal access to the gallbladder.
The surgeon grasps the gallbladder and retracts it cranially, elevating it to improve exposure of the surgical field.
Hartman's pouch is retracted upwards and to the right, allowing the surgeon to dissect the Calot's triangle.
The surgeon dissects the Calot's triangle, carefully removing fat from the area while ensuring hemostasis. The cystic duct is identified amidst the cleared tissue.
The cystic artery is carefully separated from the surrounding tissue within the dissected Calot's triangle during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon uses diathermy to remove strands of areolar tissue, providing a clear view of the cystic duct, cystic artery, and the base of the gallbladder during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector and a hook to perform the dissection, with careful application of diathermy to aid in separating the tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, with clear visualization of the cystic duct and cystic artery, ensuring proper identification and separation from surrounding structures.
A surgical clip is applied to the lower end of Hartman's pouch, followed by a small incision in the area.
The surgeon prepares to introduce the cholangiogram catheter, ensuring the area around the cystic duct is clear for catheter placement.
The surgeon flushes the cholangiogram catheter first with saline and then with contrast, preparing it for imaging within the surgical field.
The cholangiogram reveals normal anatomy with no visible stones in the common bile duct.
The surgeon clips and cuts the cystic duct, followed by clipping and cutting the cystic artery, completing the isolation of these structures.
The surgeon completes the devascularization of the gallbladder, indicating that the blood supply has been severed, preparing it for removal.
The surgeon continues the dissection cranially, separating the gallbladder from the gallbladder bed in the liver, revealing the tissue planes between these structures.
It is important to be fairly precise in this dissection in order to detect any abnormal ducts, et cetera, that may possibly emerge from the liver bed.
The surgeon identifies the ducts of Luschka, small accessory bile ducts, during the cholecystectomy procedure.

The surgeon perforates the thin-walled gallbladder towards the upper end of the gallbladder fossa, with the thin plate visible, risking bile leakage due to strain on the gallbladder.
to strain to the liver, causing extensive hemorrhage.
The surgeon lifts the gallbladder and applies appropriate tension to reveal the important dissection plane, using a hook instrument to carefully separate the tissue.
It is important to make sure that hemostasis is very, very good at the time of the initial dissection.
The surgeon addresses areas requiring hemostasis near the thin edge of the liver, applying careful attention to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon inserts a BERT bag into the abdominal cavity and places the gallbladder inside it, preparing for extraction.
This reduces the incidence of wound infection from the extraction site.
The surgeon prepares to extract the gallbladder through the umbilical port, following its placement in a BERT bag within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs a washout of the abdominal cavity, ensuring the area is clean following the gallbladder extraction.
The surgeon observes the clear fluid within the abdominal cavity, noting the absence of bile or blood.
The surgeon performs a washout of the gallbladder, ensuring it is thoroughly cleansed before extraction.

The camera is repositioned to the epigastric port, while a 10mm gallbladder grasper is inserted through the umbilical port to extract the gallbladder.
